front: Painting: a cracked/broken column replaces the subject's spine, held by a medical brace, with nails piercing the skin
Painting: symbolizes a bus accident at age 18 through nails and a shattered spinal column
Painting: commissioned by Clare Boothe Luce, shows a socialite falling from Hampshire House through clouds onto the sidewalk
back: The Suicide of Dorothy Hale (Frida Kahlo)

front: Painting: Isamu Noguchi removed the dedication from this painting commissioned by Clare Boothe Luce
back: The Suicide of Dorothy Hale (Frida Kahlo)

front: Painting: the artist included bloodstains on the frame of this painting about a socialite's death, with text at the bottom in votive style
back: The Suicide of Dorothy Hale (Frida Kahlo)

front: Painting: autobiographical work showing bare legs in a bathtub with macabre dreamlike images floating on the water
back: What the Water Gave Me (Frida Kahlo)

front: Painting: a skyscraper rising out of/emerging from a volcano appears in this bathtub painting
back: What the Water Gave Me (Frida Kahlo)

front: Painting: a dead canary/bird on top of a gnarled tree appears in this autobiographical bathtub painting
back: What the Water Gave Me (Frida Kahlo)

front: Painting: the subject wears a thorn necklace with a dead hummingbird pendant that draws blood from her neck, with a cat and monkey on her shoulders
back: Self-Portrait with Thorn Necklace and Hummingbird (Frida Kahlo)
work: Self-Portrait with Thorn Necklace and Hummingbird (1940)

front: Painting: dragonflies and butterflies hover above the subject, who is draped in thorns against a backdrop of green leaves
back: Self-Portrait with Thorn Necklace and Hummingbird (Frida Kahlo)
work: Self-Portrait with Thorn Necklace and Hummingbird (1940)

front: Painting: double self-portrait — one figure in a white Victorian gown and another in a traditional Mexican dress, connected by a blood vessel between their exposed hearts
Painting: blood drips from scissors onto a white dress; a severed vein connects two versions of the artist
Painting: votive-style work on sheet metal showing six objects (snail, orchid, pelvic bone, plaster model) connected by red cords to the artist on a hospital bed after a miscarriage
Painting: the artist's final work; sliced watermelons inscribed with the phrase 'Viva la Vida'
Painting: monkey named Fulang-Chang appears behind the subject's shoulder against green leaves; given with a mirror to Mary Sklar
Painting: Diego Rivera's face appears on the subject's forehead in self-portraits, including one with a white lace headdress of Tehuantepec
back: Self-Portrait as a Tehuana / Diego and I (Frida Kahlo)
work: Self-Portrait as a Tehuana / Diego and I

front: Painting: subject wears an oversized suit surrounded by cut hair, with a bar of music and lyrics 'Look, if I loved you it was because of your hair'
Painting: lost since 1955; depicts the artist between a tiny-headed dynamite-studded giant, a papier-mache skeleton, and a brown Nayarit figure
Painting: divided into day and night sides; the subject holds a flag reading 'tree of hope, remain strong'
back: Tree of Hope, Remain Strong (Frida Kahlo)

front: Painting: depicts a mutilated woman; title comes from the words of her murderer; bloodstains painted on the frame
Painting: depicts the Sun, images of mitosis, fertilization, historical figures, and a baby in the womb
Painting: the artist appears as an infant suckling from a bare-breasted woman wearing a ritual mask
Painting: two large hands (one with an eye in its palm) envelop the subject; a dove spreads its wings over the Earth and Moon
back: Marxism Will Give Health to the Sick (Frida Kahlo)

front: Painting: a dress suspended from a toilet and a trophy over skyscrapers; includes a lion statue and George Washington painting in a Detroit street
Artist: Andre Breton described this artist's work as 'a ribbon around a bomb' and arranged her first solo exhibition in New York in 1938
Artist: lived at the Blue House (Casa Azul) in Coyoacan, where she kept parrots and a collection of votive paintings (retablos)
Artist: her husband's affair with her sister Cristina led her to sleep with Leon Trotsky during his stay at the Blue House
Artist: her diary, kept during the last 10 years of her life, features surrealistic watercolor illustrations and was published with an introduction by Carlos Fuentes
Artist: wrote 'Feet, what do I need you for, if I have wings to fly?' below an illustration of her amputated feet in her diary
Artist: categorized as a Surrealist against her will; profiled in a condescending article titled 'Wife of the Master Mural Painter Gleefully Dabbles in Works of Art'
Artist: called Mexico City's artistic emigres, including Remedios Varo, 'the bitches of Paris'
Artist: called herself 'the Ancient Concealer' in her diary, which was written in a journal dubiously believed to have once belonged to John Keats
Painting: the artist's face on the body of a young deer pierced by nine arrows in a forest clearing; the deer was modeled by her pet named Granizo
Painting: contains the word 'carma' under a single large broken branch in the foreground
Painting: a skeleton with flowers in its heart and dynamite in its legs lies on a bunk bed above a sleeping self-portrait of the artist
Painting: shows the artist holding her husband as a nude, baby-like figure with a third eye open on his forehead
back: The Love Embrace of the Universe (Frida Kahlo)
work: The Love Embrace of the Universe (1949)

front: Painting: various insects crawl along a tightrope wrapping around the neck of a dead woman and the wrist of a masked man
back: What the Water Gave Me (Frida Kahlo)

front: Painting: the pose of two lesbian lovers in this bathtub painting is echoed in the later work Two Nudes in the Forest
back: What the Water Gave Me (Frida Kahlo)

front: Painting: the title object is also a Florence and the Machine song; two feet, one bloody and deformed, jut from grey water
back: What the Water Gave Me (Frida Kahlo)

front: Painting: Kahlo's largest work; the two children of her sister Cristina stand next to the title object; flanked by red curtains; Kahlo's arm is continuous with that of a Nayarit figurine
Painting: six objects including a floating dead baby, a disembodied pelvic bone, and an orchid are connected by red cords to the artist on a hospital bed
Artist: had an affair with Isamu Noguchi in 1936 while he was in Mexico to create a mural for her husband
Artist: played 'exquisite corpse' with Lucienne Bloch, creating an image of 'Frida' with a phallic fig leaf and 'Diego' with drooping breasts
Artist: described her husband as 'a boy bullfrog standing on his hind legs'
Artist: flower crowns and floral headdresses frequently appear in her self-portraits, along with her iconic unibrow
